question: Under s 3(1) of the Theft Act 1968, what constitutes an appropriation?

options.text: Taking property with the intention to sell it
options.feedback: The definition is wider — any assumption of the owner's rights suffices.

options.text: Physically removing property from the owner's possession
options.feedback: Physical removal need not occur — switching price labels alone can be an appropriation.

options.text: Any assumption of the rights of an owner
options.feedback: Correct. s 3(1) defines appropriation as any assumption of the rights of an owner — _R v Morris_ [1984] AC 320 confirmed that one right suffices.

options.text: Taking property without the owner's consent
options.feedback: Appropriation can occur even with consent — _R v Gomez_ [1993] AC 442 established that consent induced by fraud does not negate appropriation.

question: In _R v Gomez_ [1993] AC 442, the House of Lords held that presenting worthless cheques to obtain goods constituted appropriation. What key principle was established?

options.text: Only unauthorised takings can amount to appropriation
options.feedback: _Gomez_ explicitly rejected this and overruled _R v Lawrence_ on the issue.

options.text: An act authorised by the owner cannot be an appropriation
options.feedback: This was the dissenting view; the majority held consent obtained by fraud does not prevent appropriation.

options.text: Consent induced by fraud does not prevent an act from constituting an appropriation
options.feedback: Correct. _R v Gomez_ [1993] AC 442 — appropriation can occur even where the owner consents, if that consent was induced by deception.

options.text: Appropriation requires assuming all the rights of the owner
options.feedback: _R v Morris_ [1984] AC 320 had already established that one right of the owner suffices.

question: In _R v Hinks_ [2001] 2 AC 241, the House of Lords held that accepting a gift could constitute appropriation for theft. What factual circumstance enabled this finding?

options.text: The donor revoked the gift before it was received
options.feedback: The gift had been received and was valid in civil law — the issue was whether it could still be appropriation.

options.text: The defendant exploited a vulnerable person of low intelligence who was under undue influence
options.feedback: Correct. _R v Hinks_ [2001] 2 AC 241 held a civilly valid gift can still amount to appropriation where the defendant exploited a vulnerable donor.

options.text: The defendant used physical force to obtain the gift
options.feedback: No force was used; the donor voluntarily transferred money.

options.text: The gift was made under a legally binding contract
options.feedback: The gift was a voluntary transfer; there was no contract.

question: Under s 3(2) of the Theft Act 1968, a bona fide purchaser who later discovers goods were stolen:

options.text: Automatically commits theft by retaining the goods
options.feedback: s 3(2) provides specific protection for bona fide purchasers.

options.text: Commits theft only if they sell the goods
options.feedback: The section protects retention itself, not just sale.

options.text: Does not commit theft by retaining or dealing with the goods
options.feedback: Correct. s 3(2) — a person who purchased in good faith and for value does not commit theft by later dealing with the goods even knowing of the defect in title.

options.text: Must return the goods to avoid liability
options.feedback: s 3(2) relieves the purchaser from criminal liability regardless of return.

question: Under s 4(1) of the Theft Act 1968, which of the following constitutes 'property' capable of being stolen?

options.text: A thing in action (chose in action)
options.feedback: Correct. s 4(1) expressly includes money, personal property, real property, things in action, and other intangible property.

options.text: Confidential information
options.feedback: Confidential information is not property — _Oxford v Moss_ (1978) 68 Cr App R 183.

options.text: Wild mushrooms picked for personal consumption
options.feedback: s 4(3) provides wild plants and fungi are not property for theft unless picked for sale or reward.

options.text: An unenforceable debt
options.feedback: Only enforceable debts (things in action) are property under s 4(1).

oscoa_references: Oxford v Moss (1978) 68 Cr App R 183

question: Why was the student in _Oxford v Moss_ (1978) 68 Cr App R 183 acquitted of stealing an examination paper after reading it before the exam?

options.text: The student returned the paper, negating ITPD
options.feedback: The issue was whether the information constituted property, not ITPD.

options.text: Confidential information is not 'property' within s 4 of the Theft Act 1968
options.feedback: Correct. The court held confidential information is not intangible property capable of being stolen under the Theft Act 1968.

options.text: The student had implied consent to read the paper
options.feedback: The student clearly had no right to access the paper.

options.text: The student was not dishonest
options.feedback: Dishonesty was not the deciding issue — the case turned on the property definition.

oscoa_references: Oxford v Moss (1978) 68 Cr App R 183

question: Under s 4(2) of the Theft Act 1968, land can be stolen in which circumstance?

options.text: Where a trespasser occupies land for over 10 years
options.feedback: Adverse possession is a civil concept, not covered by s 4(2).

options.text: Where a trustee or personal representative appropriates land in breach of trust
options.feedback: Correct. s 4(2)(b) provides land can be stolen by a trustee or personal representative acting in breach of their duties.

options.text: Where an employee refuses to leave company land after termination
options.feedback: This is not one of the three specified circumstances under s 4(2).

options.text: Where a landowner sells the same plot to two different buyers
options.feedback: This is a contractual/conveyancing issue, not covered by s 4(2).

question: 'Wild mushrooms and wild plants are always capable of being stolen under the Theft Act 1968.' — True or False?

options.text: True
options.feedback: s 4(3) excludes wild mushrooms and wild plants unless picked for sale, reward, or other commercial purpose.

options.text: False
options.feedback: Correct. s 4(3) Theft Act 1968 provides wild plants, flowers, fruit, and fungi are not property for theft unless taken for sale, reward, or other commercial purpose — s 4(4).

question: Under s 5(1) of the Theft Act 1968, property is regarded as belonging to another if that person has:

options.text: Legal ownership only
options.feedback: s 5(1) is wider — it covers possession and control.

options.text: Possession, control, or any proprietary right or interest
options.feedback: Correct. s 5(1) — property belongs to any person having possession or control of it, or having any proprietary right or interest.

options.text: Beneficial ownership only
options.feedback: Possession and control are sufficient; beneficial ownership is not required.

options.text: Exclusive physical possession at the time of taking
options.feedback: Possession and control are broader concepts than exclusive physical possession.

question: In _R v Turner (No 2)_ [1971] 1 WLR 901, the defendant drove away his own car from a garage without paying for repairs. What did the Court of Appeal hold?

options.text: A person cannot steal his own property under any circumstances
options.feedback: _Turner_ established the opposite.

options.text: The defendant could not be guilty because he owned the car
options.feedback: The court rejected this because the garage had possession and control.

options.text: The garage had possession and control of the car; the defendant could steal his own property
options.feedback: Correct. _R v Turner (No 2)_ [1971] 1 WLR 901 — property can belong to another even where the defendant is the legal owner, if another has possession or control.

options.text: The defendant committed fraud, not theft
options.feedback: The court convicted for theft; the case was not reclassified as fraud.

question: In _R v Hall_ [1973] QB 126, client deposits held by a travel agent were held not to 'belong to another' under s 5(3) Theft Act 1968 because:

options.text: The deposits had been spent before the holiday was booked
options.feedback: The status of the money, not the timing of spending, was the issue.

options.text: The travel agent was under no legal obligation to treat deposits as client money or to use them in a particular way
options.feedback: Correct. _R v Hall_ [1973] QB 126 — s 5(3) requires a legal obligation to deal with property in a particular way, which was not proved on the facts.

options.text: The clients had abandoned any claim to the money
options.feedback: Abandonment was not argued or established.

options.text: The agent held the money on resulting trust for the clients
options.feedback: The court did not find a trust relationship; the contractual terms did not create one.

question: Under s 5(4) Theft Act 1968 and _Attorney-General's Reference (No 1 of 1983)_ [1985] QB 182, when money is received by mistake:

options.text: It belongs solely to the recipient until a court orders repayment
options.feedback: s 5(4) deems the money to belong to another from the moment of awareness of the mistake.

options.text: It belongs to another to the extent of the obligation to make restoration
options.feedback: Correct. s 5(4) — property obtained by another's mistake and subject to an obligation to restore belongs to the transferor. _Attorney-General's Reference (No 1 of 1983)_ [1985] QB 182 confirmed this applies to overpaid salary.

options.text: The recipient commits theft immediately upon receipt, without knowledge of the mistake
options.feedback: Knowledge of the mistake and obligation to repay is required for mens rea.

options.text: s 5(4) only applies to mistaken payments made by banks
options.feedback: s 5(4) applies broadly — illustrated by the overpaid police officer in the Attorney-General's Reference.

question: In _R v Woodman_ [1974] QB 754, scrap metal left on a fenced site was held:

options.text: To be abandoned property not capable of being stolen
options.feedback: The court held the contrary — the metal was not abandoned.

options.text: Not abandoned because the site owner had fenced the site, demonstrating control and intention to exclude others
options.feedback: Correct. _R v Woodman_ [1974] QB 754 — the company's fencing evinced control and an intention to exclude; the scrap still belonged to the company.

options.text: To belong to the scrap metal dealer who entered the site
options.feedback: The defendant was convicted of theft.

options.text: To be res nullius and therefore not subject to theft
options.feedback: The court held the property was not ownerless because the site owner had asserted possession.

question: Under s 2(1) of the Theft Act 1968, a person is not dishonest if they appropriate property in the belief that:

options.text: They will not be discovered
options.feedback: Avoiding detection is not a statutory defence under s 2(1).

options.text: They have a right in law to deprive the other of the property
options.feedback: Correct. s 2(1)(a) — a person is not dishonest if they believe they have a right in law to deprive the other of the property, for themselves or a third party.

options.text: The property is insured against loss
options.feedback: Insurance is not a statutory defence under s 2(1).

options.text: They plan to compensate the owner later
options.feedback: An intention to compensate does not negate dishonesty under the statute.

question: In _R v Holden_ [1991] Crim LR 478, what standard was held to apply to the s 2(1) beliefs?

options.text: The belief must be both genuine and reasonable
options.feedback: The court held the opposite — reasonableness is not required.

options.text: The belief need only be genuinely held, even if unreasonable
options.feedback: Correct. _R v Holden_ [1991] Crim LR 478 — the s 2(1) defences require only a genuinely held belief, not a reasonable one.

options.text: An unreasonable belief is conclusive evidence of dishonesty
options.feedback: Unreasonableness is relevant but not conclusive — _Holden_ held it does not inherently disprove the defence.

options.text: The defence requires the defendant to have sought legal advice beforehand
options.feedback: There is no requirement to have sought legal advice under s 2(1).

question: What is the correct two-stage test for dishonesty established in _Ivey v Genting Casinos (UK) Ltd_ [2017] UKSC 67?

options.text: (1) Was D's conduct dishonest by ordinary standards? (2) Did D realise it was dishonest by those standards?
options.feedback: This describes the overturned _Ghosh_ test which required a subjective second limb.

options.text: (1) What was D's actual knowledge or belief as to the facts? (2) Was D's conduct dishonest by the standards of ordinary decent people?
options.feedback: Correct. _Ivey_ test: (1) ascertain D's subjective knowledge/belief as to facts; (2) apply objective standard — dishonest by standards of ordinary decent people?

options.text: (1) Would a reasonable person consider the conduct dishonest? (2) Did D intend to cause loss?
options.feedback: Intention to cause loss is not part of the dishonesty test; this misstates _Ivey_.

options.text: (1) Did D intend to permanently deprive? (2) Was D reckless as to ownership?
options.feedback: ITPD and recklessness are separate elements of theft, not the dishonesty test.

oscoa_references: Ivey v Genting Casinos (UK) Ltd [2017] UKSC 67

question: Which case confirmed the _Ivey_ dishonesty test applies in criminal law, overturning _R v Ghosh_ [1982]?

options.text: R v Golds [2016] UKSC 61
options.feedback: _Golds_ concerned diminished responsibility, not dishonesty.

options.text: R v Barton; R v Booth [2020] EWCA Crim 575
options.feedback: Correct. _R v Barton; R v Booth_ [2020] EWCA Crim 575 confirmed _Ivey_ applies in criminal law, formally overturning the _Ghosh_ test.

options.text: R v Jogee [2016] UKSC 8
options.feedback: _Jogee_ concerned joint enterprise, not dishonesty.

options.text: R v Hayes [2015] EWCA Crim 1944
options.feedback: _Hayes_ discussed dishonesty but _Barton and Booth_ specifically confirmed _Ivey_ for criminal law.

oscoa_references: R v Barton; R v Booth [2020] EWCA Crim 575
Ivey v Genting Casinos (UK) Ltd [2017] UKSC 67

question: What is the primary tactical defence strategy post-_Ivey_ for challenging a finding of dishonesty?

options.text: Argue the defendant's conduct was morally acceptable in the defendant's community
options.feedback: _Ivey_ applies objective standards of ordinary decent people, not subjective community standards.

options.text: Argue the defendant did not consider their conduct dishonest
options.feedback: Under _Ivey_, the defendant's opinion on the dishonesty of their own conduct is irrelevant.

options.text: Focus on limb (1) — challenge the prosecution's account of the defendant's actual knowledge or belief as to the facts
options.feedback: Correct. _Ivey_ [2017] UKSC 67 — the purely objective limb (2) is hard to challenge; the tactic is to dispute what D actually knew or believed about the factual circumstances under limb (1).

options.text: Argue the Ghosh test should still apply
options.feedback: _R v Barton; R v Booth_ [2020] EWCA Crim 575 confirmed _Ghosh_ is no longer good law.

oscoa_references: Ivey v Genting Casinos (UK) Ltd [2017] UKSC 67
R v Barton; R v Booth [2020] EWCA Crim 575

question: Under s 6 of the Theft Act 1968, an intention to permanently deprive can be established where the defendant:

options.text: Intends to return the property within a reasonable time with its value intact
options.feedback: Mere borrowing where the property retains its practical value on return does not establish ITPD.

options.text: Treats the thing as their own to dispose of regardless of the owner's rights
options.feedback: Correct. s 6 extends ITPD to include treating property as one's own to dispose of regardless of the owner's rights, or borrowing equivalent to outright taking.

options.text: Takes the property accidentally without realising
options.feedback: There must be an intention; accidental taking lacks ITPD.

options.text: Takes property intending to return it after two weeks
options.feedback: A short borrowing where property's value is not exhausted does not constitute ITPD — _R v Lloyd_ [1985] QB 829.

question: In _R v Lloyd_ [1985] QB 829, a film projectionist borrowed films to make pirate copies and returned them. Why was there no ITPD?

options.text: The defendant intended to return the films and their practical value was not diminished
options.feedback: Correct. The borrowing was not equivalent to outright taking — the films retained their full practical value upon return.

options.text: The defendant lacked dishonesty because he returned the films
options.feedback: Dishonesty and ITPD are separate elements — the case turned on ITPD.

options.text: The films were not property capable of being stolen
options.feedback: Physical items with copyright are still property for theft purposes.

options.text: The defendant had the owner's implied consent
options.feedback: The employer did not consent to the films being taken for piracy.

question: In _R v Velumyl_ [1989] Crim LR 299, the defendant took cash from the safe intending to replace it with equivalent notes. What was held?

options.text: The defendant lacked ITPD because he intended to return equivalent money
options.feedback: The court held the opposite — intending to return different notes established ITPD.

options.text: The defendant had ITPD because he intended to return different notes, not the exact currency taken
options.feedback: Correct. _R v Velumyl_ [1989] Crim LR 299 — replacing specific bank notes with equivalent notes still constitutes ITPD; D treated the notes as his own.

options.text: The defendant committed fraud, not theft
options.feedback: The court upheld the theft conviction.

options.text: The defendant lacked ITPD because the total value was the same
options.feedback: The Court of Appeal rejected this — the specific notes were not returned.

question: In _R v Fernandes_ [1996] 1 Cr App R 175, a solicitor transferred client money into a risky investment. What did the court hold?

options.text: The solicitor lacked ITPD because he intended to generate returns
options.feedback: The court held the risk-taking conduct fell within s 6.

options.text: Putting property beyond the owner's reach by dealing with it in a way that risks its loss falls within 'disposing of' under s 6
options.feedback: Correct. _R v Fernandes_ [1996] 1 Cr App R 175 — placing property at risk beyond the client's control constitutes treating it as one's own to dispose of.

options.text: The solicitor was liable only under the Fraud Act 2006
options.feedback: The Fraud Act 2006 was not yet in force; the conviction was under the Theft Act 1968.

options.text: There was no ITPD because the solicitor intended to account for the money later
options.feedback: The court rejected this — money put at risk without consent establishes ITPD.

oscoa_references: R v Fernandes [1996] 1 Cr App R 175

question: Under s 8 of the Theft Act 1968, when must force be used for robbery?

options.text: At any time before, during, or after the stealing
options.feedback: Force after the stealing is complete is not covered by s 8.

options.text: Immediately before or at the time of stealing, and for the purpose of stealing
options.feedback: Correct. s 8(1) requires force be used immediately before or at the time of stealing, for the purpose of stealing.

options.text: Within 24 hours of the stealing
options.feedback: There is no 24-hour window — the timing is 'immediately before or at the time'.

options.text: Only at the precise moment property is taken
options.feedback: Force immediately before the stealing is also sufficient.

question: Why was the defendant in _R v Robinson_ [1977] Crim LR 173 acquitted of robbery despite using force to take money?

options.text: The force used was insufficient for robbery
options.feedback: The force was sufficient; a foundational element of theft was missing.

options.text: He genuinely believed he was owed the money, so he lacked dishonesty; without theft there can be no robbery
options.feedback: Correct. _R v Robinson_ [1977] Crim LR 173 — all elements of theft must be proved for robbery. If D lacks dishonesty, there is no theft and therefore no robbery.

options.text: The victim consented to the taking
options.feedback: The victim did not consent; force was used.

options.text: The money was not property capable of being stolen
options.feedback: Money is clearly property; the issue was dishonesty.

question: In _R v Dawson and James_ (1976) 64 Cr App R 170, what degree of force was held sufficient for robbery?

options.text: Force must cause actual bodily harm
options.feedback: The court held force need only be minimal; ABH is not required.

options.text: Force need only be minimal; a nudge can be sufficient
options.feedback: Correct. _R v Dawson and James_ (1976) — 'force' is a word in ordinary use; the jury may find a nudge or push constitutes force.

options.text: Force must be such that the victim fears for their life
options.feedback: No such high threshold was established.

options.text: Force is measured by the value of property taken
options.feedback: The value of property is irrelevant to whether force was used.

oscoa_references: R v Dawson and James (1976) 64 Cr App R 170

question: 'Wrenching a handbag from a victim's hand is insufficient to constitute force for robbery.' — True or False?

options.text: True
options.feedback: _R v Clouden_ [1987] Crim LR 56 held wrenching a bag from the victim's hand constituted sufficient force.

options.text: False
options.feedback: Correct. _R v Clouden_ [1987] Crim LR 56 — wrenching a bag from the victim's hand constituted sufficient force for robbery under s 8.

question: What is the distinction between s 9(1)(a) and s 9(1)(b) burglary?

options.text: s 9(1)(a) applies to dwellings; s 9(1)(b) applies to commercial premises
options.feedback: The distinction is about timing of intent, not type of building.

options.text: s 9(1)(a) requires intent to commit an offence at point of entry; s 9(1)(b) requires actual commission of the offence (or attempt) after entry as a trespasser
options.feedback: Correct. s 9(1)(a): entry with intent to steal/inflict GBH/cause unlawful damage. s 9(1)(b): having entered as trespasser, steals/attempts to steal or inflicts/attempts to inflict GBH.

options.text: There is no meaningful distinction between them
options.feedback: They are distinct offences with different elements and timing requirements.

options.text: s 9(1)(a) is indictment only; s 9(1)(b) is either way
options.feedback: Both are triable either way.

question: Which statement correctly states the law on what constitutes 'entry' for burglary?

options.text: The defendant's entire body must cross the threshold
options.feedback: _R v Brown_ [1985] and _R v Ryan_ [1996] establish that partial entry can suffice.

options.text: Entry must be effective — partial entry of the body can suffice
options.feedback: Correct. _R v Brown_ [1985] Crim LR 212 — leaning through a window constitutes entry; _R v Ryan_ [1996] Crim LR 320 — body partially through a window constitutes entry.

options.text: Entry is established only once the defendant touches the floor inside
options.feedback: The cases show touching the floor is not required.

options.text: Using an instrument to gain access never constitutes entry
options.feedback: An instrument used as an extension of the person can constitute entry.

question: In _R v Jones and Smith_ [1976] 1 WLR 672, a son with general permission to enter his father's house entered intending to steal a TV. What did the court hold?

options.text: The son was not a trespasser because he had general permission
options.feedback: The court held the opposite — general permission does not protect against trespass for a prohibited purpose.

options.text: The son was a trespasser because he entered in excess of the permission granted
options.feedback: Correct. _R v Jones and Smith_ [1976] 1 WLR 672 — entering with intent to steal exceeds any implied licence; the son was a trespasser.

options.text: The son was not a burglar because the house belonged to his father
options.feedback: Family relationship does not preclude burglary.

options.text: Permission was revoked only after he removed the TV
options.feedback: The court held he was a trespasser at the moment of entry because of his intent.

oscoa_references: R v Jones and Smith [1976] 1 WLR 672

question: In _R v Walkington_ [1979] 1 WLR 1169, why was a customer who went behind a till counter in a store during opening hours convicted of burglary?

options.text: He entered the store with the intention of stealing
options.feedback: Entry to the store during opening hours was lawful; the trespass was to a specific area.

options.text: The till area constituted a 'part of a building' to which the public had no access; entry was as a trespasser
options.feedback: Correct. _R v Walkington_ [1979] 1 WLR 1169 — a restricted part of a building can be the subject of burglary when the defendant knew he had no permission to enter that area.

options.text: He committed criminal damage to the till
options.feedback: No criminal damage was at issue.

options.text: He remained in the store after closing time
options.feedback: The store was open; trespass related to the restricted area.

question: Under s 10 Theft Act 1968, aggravated burglary requires having with you at the time of the burglary:

options.text: Property stolen from the building
options.feedback: Stolen property is evidence of the underlying burglary, not the aggravating factor.

options.text: A firearm, imitation firearm, weapon of offence, or explosive
options.feedback: Correct. s 10 specifies WIFE items — weapon of offence, imitation firearm, firearm, or explosive — at the time of burglary.

options.text: A getaway vehicle
options.feedback: A getaway vehicle is not specified in s 10 as an aggravating item.

options.text: An accomplice acting as a lookout
options.feedback: Having an accomplice does not alone constitute aggravated burglary under s 10.

question: Under s 2 of the Fraud Act 2006, a person commits fraud by false representation if they:

options.text: Make a false representation recklessly as to its truth
options.feedback: Recklessness is not the standard — knowledge is required.

options.text: Dishonestly make a false representation, intending to make a gain or cause loss or expose another to risk of loss
options.feedback: Correct. s 2: (1) dishonestly (2) makes a false representation (3) intending to make a gain or cause loss or risk of loss.

options.text: Make a false representation regardless of dishonesty
options.feedback: Dishonesty is an essential element under s 2.

options.text: Make a representation which is false in fact, whether or not they know it
options.feedback: The defendant must know the representation is or might be untrue or misleading.

question: In _R v Lambie_ [1982] AC 449, a woman used her credit card knowing her account was over its limit. What form did the false representation take?

options.text: An express statement that the account had sufficient funds
options.feedback: The defendant made no express statement about her account balance.

options.text: An implied representation that she had the bank's authority to use the card and the bank would honour the transaction
options.feedback: Correct. _R v Lambie_ [1982] AC 449 — presenting a credit card implies a representation of authority from the card issuer.

options.text: A false representation as to her identity
options.feedback: The card was her own; there was no identity issue.

options.text: A representation to a machine, which cannot constitute fraud
options.feedback: s 2(4) Fraud Act 2006 and _Lambie_ confirm representations can be made to machines.

question: A representation is 'false' under s 2(2) Fraud Act 2006 if:

options.text: It is factually incorrect, regardless of the defendant's knowledge
options.feedback: Mens rea is required — the defendant must know the representation is or might be untrue or misleading.

options.text: It is untrue or misleading and the defendant knows it is or might be untrue or misleading
options.feedback: Correct. s 2(2): a representation is false if (a) it is untrue or misleading, and (b) the person making it knows or might know it is.

options.text: It causes financial loss to another person
options.feedback: Loss is not required for the representation to be false — it relates to the defendant's intention.

options.text: A reasonable person would consider it false
options.feedback: s 2(2) uses a subjective knowledge test, not a reasonable person standard.

question: Under s 2(3) Fraud Act 2006, a representation may be as to:

options.text: Fact only
options.feedback: s 2(3) extends beyond matters of fact.

options.text: Fact or law only
options.feedback: s 2(3) also includes representations as to a state of mind.

options.text: Fact, law, or state of mind
options.feedback: Correct. s 2(3) provides a representation may be as to fact, law, or state of mind — including the defendant's own intentions.

options.text: Financial matters only
options.feedback: The Fraud Act 2006 is not limited to financial representations.

question: In _R v Cornelius_ [2012] EWCA Crim 528, what did the Court of Appeal clarify?

options.text: A representation is not false if the defendant genuinely believed it to be true, even if mistaken
options.feedback: Correct. _R v Cornelius_ [2012] EWCA Crim 528 — the prosecution must prove D knew the representation was or might be false; genuine mistake negates this.

options.text: Negligent representations automatically satisfy the mens rea
options.feedback: Negligence is insufficient — knowledge is required.

options.text: A representation to a machine can never constitute fraud
options.feedback: s 2(4) confirms representations can be made to machines.

options.text: The Fraud Act 2006 does not apply to representations by omission
options.feedback: s 3 deals with omissions separately; _Cornelius_ concerned s 2.

question: Under s 3 of the Fraud Act 2006, a person commits fraud by failing to disclose information only where:

options.text: The failure causes financial loss to another
options.feedback: Loss is not required — risk of loss suffices, and the duty to disclose is the essential element.

options.text: There is a legal duty to disclose the information
options.feedback: Correct. s 3 requires (1) dishonestly (2) failing to disclose (3) where there is a legal duty (4) intending to make gain or cause loss.

options.text: The information relates to a criminal offence
options.feedback: The duty can arise from any source — statute, contract, fiduciary relationship, or custom.

options.text: The defendant is in a regulated profession
options.feedback: s 3 applies broadly, not only to regulated professionals.

question: In _R v Valujevs_ [2014] EWCA Crim 2888, what position did the defendant occupy for s 4 abuse of position?

options.text: A company director who misappropriated funds
options.feedback: _Valujevs_ concerned a different role.

options.text: A gangmaster who was expected to safeguard workers' financial interests but deducted wages
options.feedback: Correct. _R v Valujevs_ [2014] EWCA Crim 2888 — as a gangmaster, D occupied a position expected to safeguard workers' financial interests.

options.text: A solicitor who transferred client funds without authorisation
options.feedback: This describes _R v Fernandes_ [1996], not _Valujevs_.

options.text: An accountant who filed fraudulent tax returns
options.feedback: This is not the fact pattern in _Valujevs_.

question: In _R v Razoq_ [2020] EWCA Crim 1737, the Court of Appeal confirmed a legal duty to disclose may arise from:

options.text: Statute only
options.feedback: The court confirmed a wider range of sources.

options.text: Statute, contract, fiduciary relationship, or custom
options.feedback: Correct. _R v Razoq_ [2020] EWCA Crim 1737 — a legal duty to disclose under s 3 may arise from statute, contract, fiduciary relationship, or the customs of a particular trade or profession.

options.text: The defendant's personal moral code
options.feedback: Moral obligations are insufficient — a legal duty is required.

options.text: The victim's reasonable expectation of disclosure
options.feedback: Reasonable expectation alone does not create a legal duty.

question: Under s 4 Fraud Act 2006, a person 'occupies a position' if expected to:

options.text: Supervise other employees
options.feedback: The expectation relates to safeguarding financial interests, not general supervision.

options.text: Safeguard, or not act against, the financial interests of another
options.feedback: Correct. s 4(1)(a) — the position is one expected to safeguard, or not to act against, the financial interests of another.

options.text: Hold a position of trust in any public body
options.feedback: s 4 is not limited to public bodies.

options.text: Receive payment for services rendered
options.feedback: Receiving payment alone does not create a position for s 4 purposes.

question: Under s 11 of the Fraud Act 2006, obtaining services dishonestly requires that the services are obtained:

options.text: Without paying for them
options.feedback: Non-payment is not an element; the act focuses on dishonest obtaining.

options.text: By a dishonest act, knowing the defendant is not entitled to obtain them
options.feedback: Correct. s 11: obtaining services by a dishonest act, where services are made available on payment basis, knowing not entitled to obtain them.

options.text: By making a false representation to the service provider
options.feedback: A false representation is one way but not a required element — the act need only be dishonest.

options.text: From a public authority only
options.feedback: s 11 is not limited to public authorities.

question: s 11 of the Fraud Act 2006 replaced which predecessor offence?

options.text: Theft — obtaining services by appropriation
options.feedback: Services cannot be appropriated; the Theft Act 1968 did not cover this.

options.text: Theft Act 1978, s 1 — obtaining services by deception
options.feedback: Correct. s 11 Fraud Act 2006 replaced s 1 Theft Act 1978, which criminalised obtaining services by deception.

options.text: Common law offence of cheating the revenue
options.feedback: Cheating the revenue is a separate common law offence that survives the Fraud Act 2006.

options.text: Theft Act 1968, s 15 — obtaining property by deception
options.feedback: s 15 dealt with obtaining property; s 11 deals with obtaining services.

question: 'Under s 11 Fraud Act 2006, merely failing to pay for services received constitutes obtaining services dishonestly.' — True or False?

options.text: True
options.feedback: s 11 requires a dishonest act to obtain the services — mere non-payment after honest receipt is not enough.

options.text: False
options.feedback: Correct. s 11 requires a dishonest act by which services are obtained. Failing to pay after honest receipt is not within s 11.

question: Under s 327 of POCA 2002, which of the following constitutes a principal money laundering offence?

options.text: Failing to report suspicious activity to the NCA
options.feedback: This is a separate offence under ss 330-332 (failure to disclose).

options.text: Concealing, disguising, converting, transferring, or removing criminal property from the UK
options.feedback: Correct. s 327 creates the offence of concealing, disguising, converting, transferring, or removing criminal property.

options.text: Tipping off a person that an investigation is underway
options.feedback: This is an offence under s 333A (tipping off), not s 327.

options.text: Failing to conduct customer due diligence
options.feedback: This is a regulatory breach under MLR 2017, not a POCA principal offence.

question: Under s 328 of POCA 2002, the 'arrangements' offence criminalises:

options.text: Only arrangements involving banks and financial institutions
options.feedback: s 328 applies broadly, not only to financial institutions.

options.text: Entering into or becoming concerned in an arrangement which facilitates the acquisition, retention, use, or control of criminal property
options.feedback: Correct. s 328 captures those who facilitate money laundering through arrangements, even if they do not handle the criminal property themselves.

options.text: Reporting an arrangement to the NCA without obtaining consent
options.feedback: Reporting to the NCA is part of the authorised disclosure defence, not the offence.

options.text: Only arrangements involving property worth over £10,000
options.feedback: There is no minimum value threshold for s 328.

question: Under s 340 of POCA 2002, 'criminal property' is defined as property that:

options.text: Has been the subject of a criminal conviction
options.feedback: A conviction is not required — the property need only constitute a benefit from criminal conduct.

options.text: Constitutes or represents a person's benefit from criminal conduct, where the alleged offender knows or suspects it is such
options.feedback: Correct. s 340 defines criminal property as property constituting/representing a benefit from criminal conduct, with knowledge or suspicion on the part of the alleged offender.

options.text: Was acquired through civil wrongs
options.feedback: Criminal conduct is required; civil wrongs do not trigger POCA.

options.text: Is cash or other currency only
options.feedback: Criminal property includes all forms of property, not just cash.

question: What defence is available under POCA 2002 to the principal money laundering offences?

options.text: The defendant was acting under duress
options.feedback: Duress is a general common law defence, not the specific statutory defence.

options.text: The defendant made an authorised disclosure to the NCA and obtained appropriate consent
options.feedback: Correct. An authorised disclosure (DAML SAR) to the NCA, with appropriate consent, provides a defence to the principal ML offences under ss 327(2)(a), 328(2)(a), 329(2)(a).

options.text: The defendant returned the criminal property before being detected
options.feedback: Returning property does not undo the commission of the offence.

options.text: The defendant was unaware because they deliberately avoided making enquiries
options.feedback: Wilful blindness does not provide a defence; suspicion may suffice.

question: Under s 335 of POCA 2002, after an authorised disclosure, how many working days does the NCA have before deemed consent applies?

options.text: 3 working days
options.feedback: The notice period is 7 working days.

options.text: 7 working days
options.feedback: Correct. s 335 provides a 7-working-day moratorium period. The NCA may apply to extend this by up to 31 days via court order.

options.text: 14 working days
options.feedback: The initial period is 7 working days, though extensions are possible.

options.text: 28 calendar days
options.feedback: The period is in working days, not calendar days, and is 7 initially.

question: Under s 330 of POCA 2002, who in the regulated sector commits an offence by failing to disclose money laundering?

options.text: Any person who suspects money laundering, regardless of profession
options.feedback: s 330 applies specifically to those in the regulated sector.

options.text: A person in the regulated sector who knows, suspects, or has reasonable grounds to know or suspect money laundering
options.feedback: Correct. s 330 imposes an obligation including an objective 'reasonable grounds to know or suspect' standard.

options.text: Only nominated officers within a firm
options.feedback: The nominated officer offence is under s 331; s 330 applies to regulated sector employees.

options.text: Only solicitors and accountants
options.feedback: The regulated sector is broader — includes financial institutions, estate agents, casinos, and others.

question: How does s 332 (non-regulated sector) differ from s 330 (regulated sector) for failure to disclose?

options.text: s 332 requires proof of intent; s 330 uses an objective 'reasonable grounds to suspect' standard
options.feedback: Correct. s 332 requires actual knowledge/suspicion and an intentional failure. s 330 is broader — it includes an objective standard.

options.text: s 332 carries a higher maximum sentence
options.feedback: Both carry a maximum of 5 years imprisonment.

options.text: s 332 applies only to transactions above £10,000
options.feedback: There is no de minimis threshold.

options.text: There is no distinction — both sections are identical
options.feedback: The sections have materially different mens rea requirements.

question: Which can constitute a 'reasonable excuse' defence under s 330(6)(a) POCA 2002?

options.text: Fear of reprisal from the suspected money launderer
options.feedback: Fear of reprisal alone is not typically accepted.

options.text: Information came to the person in circumstances subject to legal professional privilege
options.feedback: Correct. s 330(10) preserves legal professional privilege. Information obtained in privileged circumstances need not be disclosed.

options.text: The transaction was relatively low in value
options.feedback: Low value does not alone constitute a reasonable excuse.

options.text: The discloser was too busy to make a report
options.feedback: Operational pressure is not a reasonable excuse.

question: How can legal professional privilege be deployed as a defence to a failure to disclose charge under s 330?

options.text: By claiming privilege over all client communications
options.feedback: Privilege is not blanket — it applies where communications are for the dominant purpose of legal advice or contemplated litigation.

options.text: By demonstrating the information came in privileged circumstances — s 330(10) preserves privilege as a complete defence
options.feedback: Correct. s 330(10) POCA 2002 — where information is obtained in privileged circumstances, the regulated professional is not required to disclose.

options.text: By waiving privilege and making full disclosure to the NCA after prosecution
options.feedback: Post-prosecution disclosure is not a defence; the offence is committed at the time of failure.

options.text: By arguing GDPR overrides POCA disclosure obligations
options.feedback: GDPR does not override POCA; legal professional privilege is the relevant protection.

question: Under s 333A POCA 2002 (as amended), tipping off occurs when a person:

options.text: Makes a SAR to the NCA that is later found unnecessary
options.feedback: Making a SAR is not tipping off — it is encouraged.

options.text: Discloses information likely to prejudice a money laundering investigation
options.feedback: Correct. s 333A criminalises disclosing information likely to prejudice an ongoing or prospective ML investigation.

options.text: Accepts payment from a client suspected of money laundering
options.feedback: This is an acquisition offence under s 329, not tipping off.

options.text: Reports suspicious activity to a person higher up within the same firm
options.feedback: Disclosures within the same undertaking are permitted under s 333B.

question: Under s 333B POCA 2002, a disclosure does not constitute tipping off if made:

options.text: To the client who is the subject of the investigation
options.feedback: Disclosure to the subject is precisely what tipping off prevents.

options.text: Within the same undertaking to a nominated officer or MLRO
options.feedback: Correct. s 333B permits disclosures within the same undertaking, enabling internal escalation without committing tipping off.

options.text: To any other professional in the same industry
options.feedback: The exception is limited to within the same undertaking.

options.text: To the media in the public interest
options.feedback: Disclosure to the media is not within the s 333B exception.

question: What safe communication strategy avoids tipping off while allowing a professional to advise a client?

options.text: Inform the client that a SAR has been filed and explain its contents
options.feedback: This is precisely what tipping off prohibits.

options.text: Inform the client of legal obligations generally without linking to any specific SAR or investigation
options.feedback: Correct. General discussion of obligations without referencing any specific SAR or investigation avoids tipping off while fulfilling professional duties.

options.text: Refuse to communicate with the client after filing a SAR
options.feedback: Complete withdrawal is not required — safe, non-prejudicial communication is possible.

options.text: Seek the NCA's permission before any communication with the client
options.feedback: NCA permission is not required for general communications.

question: Under MLR 2017, regs 27-30, customer due diligence requires a relevant person to:

options.text: Only verify the customer's name and address
options.feedback: CDD requires more than name and address verification.

options.text: Identify the customer, verify identity, identify the beneficial owner, and assess the purpose and intended nature of the business relationship
options.feedback: Correct. MLR 2017, regs 27-30 require identification and verification of customer and beneficial owner, plus assessment of purpose and intended nature.

options.text: Only verify the customer's source of funds
options.feedback: Source of funds checks are part of EDD, not baseline CDD.

options.text: Conduct CDD only when opening a bank account
options.feedback: CDD is required in broader circumstances, including occasional transactions and when ML suspicion arises.

oscoa_references: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, regs 27-30

question: Simplified due diligence under reg 37 MLR 2017 may be applied where:

options.text: The customer is a politically exposed person
options.feedback: PEPs require enhanced due diligence, not simplified.

options.text: The business relationship or transaction presents a low degree of risk of money laundering
options.feedback: Correct. SDD under reg 37 is permitted only where there is low risk, following an adequate assessment.

options.text: The customer operates in a high-risk third country
options.feedback: High-risk jurisdictions require EDD, not SDD.

options.text: The customer makes a one-off transaction exceeding €15,000
options.feedback: A high-value one-off transaction typically triggers full CDD, not SDD.

oscoa_references: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, reg 37

question: Under reg 35 MLR 2017, which of the following requires enhanced due diligence as a PEP?

options.text: Any person who was a Member of Parliament over 10 years ago
options.feedback: PEP status generally applies to those currently entrusted with prominent public functions, and for 12 months after.

options.text: Family members and known close associates of a PEP
options.feedback: Correct. reg 35(5)-(7) extends EDD requirements to family members (spouse, children, parents) and any person known to be a close associate of a PEP.

options.text: Any employee of a government department
options.feedback: Junior government employees are not PEPs — the definition is limited to prominent public functions.

options.text: Only heads of state and government ministers
options.feedback: The definition includes MPs, supreme court judges, and senior executives of state-owned enterprises.

oscoa_references: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, reg 35

question: Enhanced due diligence under reg 33 MLR 2017 is required where:

options.text: The customer is a UK-registered public limited company
options.feedback: PLCs are generally lower risk and may fall within SDD.

options.text: The customer is established in a high-risk third country
options.feedback: Correct. reg 33 requires EDD where the customer is established in a high-risk third country, the transaction is complex/unusually large, or the customer is a PEP.

options.text: The transaction is under £1,000
options.feedback: Low-value transactions do not automatically require EDD.

options.text: The customer is an existing client with an established relationship
options.feedback: Existing relationships require ongoing monitoring, but EDD is triggered by specific risk factors.

oscoa_references: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, reg 33

question: Under reg 39 MLR 2017, reliance on third-party CDD means:

options.text: The relying firm is discharged from all CDD responsibility
options.feedback: Ultimate responsibility remains with the relying firm.

options.text: The relying firm remains ultimately responsible; must obtain CDD information from the third party on request without delay
options.feedback: Correct. reg 39 permits third-party reliance but ultimate responsibility stays with the relying firm, which must promptly obtain the information upon request.

options.text: Only banks may be relied upon for third-party CDD
options.feedback: A range of regulated entities are eligible third parties, not only banks.

options.text: The relying firm need not verify the third party's regulatory status
options.feedback: The firm must ensure the third party is adequately regulated and supervised.

oscoa_references: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, reg 39

question: What argument can challenge whether a person qualifies as a 'known close associate' of a PEP under reg 35 MLR 2017?

options.text: PEP status expires after the PEP leaves office, so EDD is never required
options.feedback: While PEP status does expire, this goes to the PEP themselves, not the close associate definition.

options.text: The term 'known' introduces a knowledge requirement — if the firm was not actually aware of the close association, EDD may not have been triggered
options.feedback: Correct. The word 'known' in reg 35(7) creates an interpretative gap: EDD obligations arise only where the association is actually known to the firm, not where it might have been discovered through reasonable enquiry.

options.text: Only UK PEPs are within the scope of MLR 2017
options.feedback: MLR 2017 applies to all PEPs, domestic or foreign.

options.text: Family members are excluded if the PEP has died
options.feedback: The Regulations do not contain a death exclusion for family members.

oscoa_references: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, reg 35

question: Under reg 18 MLR 2017, a relevant person must:

options.text: Conduct CDD on every person who walks into the premises
options.feedback: CDD is required for customers entering a business relationship, not every visitor.

options.text: Carry out a written firm-wide risk assessment to identify and assess the risk of money laundering and terrorist financing
options.feedback: Correct. reg 18 requires a written firm-wide risk assessment identifying and assessing ML/TF risks.

options.text: Submit quarterly reports to the NCA
options.feedback: There is no blanket quarterly reporting — SARs are filed as suspicious activity arises.

options.text: Employ at least one full-time compliance officer per 50 employees
options.feedback: MLR 2017 contains no such staffing ratio.

oscoa_references: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, reg 18

question: Under reg 21 MLR 2017, what does a relevant person need to do regarding governance?

options.text: Appoint a board-level or senior management Money Laundering Reporting Officer (MLRO)
options.feedback: Correct. reg 21 requires appointment of an MLRO who is a member of the board or senior management.

options.text: Outsource all AML functions to an external consultant
options.feedback: External support may be used but the appointment responsibility rests with the firm.

options.text: Register the MLRO with Companies House
options.feedback: MLROs should be notified to the firm's supervisory authority, not Companies House.

options.text: Appoint two MLROs to ensure segregation of duties
options.feedback: MLR 2017 does not require dual appointments.

oscoa_references: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, reg 21

question: 'Under reg 24 MLR 2017, relevant persons must provide AML training only to compliance or legal staff.' — True or False?

options.text: True
options.feedback: reg 24 requires training for all relevant employees, not only compliance or legal staff.

options.text: False
options.feedback: Correct. reg 24 requires all relevant employees be made aware of ML/TF law and regularly given training.

oscoa_references: Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017, reg 24

question: Under s 1 of the Bribery Act 2010, a person is guilty of bribing another if they offer, promise, or give a financial or other advantage:

options.text: Only to a public official holding government office
options.feedback: s 1 applies broadly to any person; bribery of foreign public officials is under s 6.

options.text: Intending to induce or reward improper performance, or knowing or believing acceptance would constitute improper performance
options.feedback: Correct. s 1 — the offence is offering/promising/giving an advantage linked to improper performance of a relevant function or activity.

options.text: To any person, provided the advantage exceeds £500
options.feedback: There is no de minimis threshold in the Bribery Act 2010.

options.text: Only where the recipient accepts the advantage
options.feedback: The offence is complete upon offering, promising, or giving — acceptance is not required.

question: Under s 2 of the Bribery Act 2010, the offence of being bribed involves:

options.text: Actually receiving a financial advantage only
options.feedback: The offence is broader — requesting or agreeing to receive is sufficient.

options.text: Requesting, agreeing to receive, or accepting a financial advantage intending improper performance or where the request itself constitutes improper performance
options.feedback: Correct. s 2 captures the recipient side — requesting, agreeing to receive, or accepting with the requisite improper performance intention.

options.text: Receiving a payment the recipient knows to be unlawful
options.feedback: Knowledge of unlawfulness as such is not an element; the test is improper performance.

options.text: Only passive receipt of a bribe where no active steps are taken
options.feedback: Requesting and agreeing to receive are active steps captured by s 2.

question: How does s 6 (bribing a foreign public official) differ from the general s 1 bribery offence?

options.text: s 6 requires proof of dishonesty, unlike s 1
options.feedback: Neither s 1 nor s 6 requires proof of dishonesty.

options.text: s 6 is narrower — the advantage must be intended to influence the FPO in their official capacity and to obtain/retain business or a business advantage
options.feedback: Correct. s 6 requires intention to influence in the FPO's capacity and to obtain/retain business, but does not require improper performance as an element.

options.text: s 6 applies only to officials of EU member states
options.feedback: s 6 applies to foreign public officials of any country outside the UK.

options.text: s 6 does not require any connection to business
options.feedback: The business advantage element is a defining feature of s 6, distinguishing it from s 1.

question: Under ss 3-5 of the Bribery Act 2010, improper performance means performance in breach of:

options.text: A criminal statute only
options.feedback: The expectation can arise from non-statutory sources.

options.text: A relevant expectation — that the function be performed in good faith, impartially, or in accordance with a position of trust
options.feedback: Correct. ss 3-5 define improper performance by reference to breach of expectation of good faith, impartiality, or trust.

options.text: Any company policy, regardless of formality
options.feedback: Informal policies may not rise to a 'relevant expectation' under ss 3-5.

options.text: The recipient's personal moral code
options.feedback: The test is what a reasonable person in the UK would expect, not individual morality.

question: 'A conviction under the Bribery Act 2010 requires proof of dishonesty.' — True or False?

options.text: True
options.feedback: The Bribery Act 2010 was deliberately drafted without a dishonesty requirement.

options.text: False
options.feedback: Correct. The Bribery Act 2010 does not require proof of dishonesty. The focus is on offering/giving an advantage linked to improper performance.

question: Under s 7 of the Bribery Act 2010, a commercial organisation commits an offence if:

options.text: A director personally pays a bribe
options.feedback: This may engage personal liability under s 1 or 2; s 7 is about associated persons.

options.text: A person associated with the organisation bribes another intending to obtain/retain business or a business advantage for the organisation
options.feedback: Correct. s 7 creates strict liability corporate offence when an associated person bribes intending to benefit the organisation.

options.text: The organisation fails to report bribery to the SFO within 30 days
options.feedback: There is no statutory reporting deadline; the offence is triggered by the bribery itself.

options.text: The organisation's turnover exceeds £36 million
options.feedback: s 7 applies to all commercial organisations, regardless of turnover.

question: What is the statutory defence for a commercial organisation charged under s 7 Bribery Act 2010?

options.text: The organisation had no actual knowledge of the bribery
options.feedback: No actual knowledge is required for s 7 liability — the defence is based on procedures.

options.text: The organisation had adequate procedures in place designed to prevent bribery by associated persons
options.feedback: Correct. s 7(2) provides the 'adequate procedures' defence — a complete defence if proved.

options.text: The bribe was of negligible value
options.feedback: There is no de minimis exception in the Bribery Act 2010.

options.text: The associated person was acting outside the scope of their authority
options.feedback: s 7 extends to associated persons regardless of authority; the defence is procedures-based.

question: The MOJ Guidance on the Bribery Act 2010 identifies six principles for adequate procedures. Which is one of them?

options.text: Mandatory polygraph testing for all employees
options.feedback: Polygraphs are not part of the MOJ Guidance.

options.text: Proportionate procedures
options.feedback: Correct. The six principles are: (1) proportionate procedures, (2) top-level commitment, (3) risk assessment, (4) due diligence, (5) communication (including training), and (6) monitoring and review.

options.text: Mandatory external audit by an SFO-approved firm
options.feedback: External audit is recommended in monitoring/review but not mandatory.

options.text: Mandatory dismissal for employees who fail to complete anti-bribery training
options.feedback: No mandatory dismissal requirement exists.

oscoa_references: Ministry of Justice, The Bribery Act 2010: Guidance (2011)

question: What is the most effective tactical approach to ensure an organisation can rely on the 'adequate procedures' defence?

options.text: Implement a written anti-bribery policy and circulate it once to all staff
options.feedback: One-time circulation is insufficient — the defence requires ongoing implementation, monitoring, and review.

options.text: Implement and document all six MOJ principles supported by an independent external compliance audit
options.feedback: Correct. s 7(2) defence is fact-sensitive; satisfying all six MOJ principles with documented evidence and independent audit provides the strongest foundation.

options.text: Rely on the fact that no bribery has been detected to date
options.feedback: Absence of detected bribery does not prove adequate procedures were in place.

options.text: Delegate all anti-bribery compliance to a junior HR officer
options.feedback: Top-level commitment (principle 2) requires board-level ownership.

oscoa_references: Ministry of Justice, The Bribery Act 2010: Guidance (2011)

question: How does the UK Bribery Act 2010 treat facilitation payments compared to the US Foreign Corrupt Practices Act?

options.text: Both permit facilitation payments where local custom expects them
options.feedback: This is not correct for the UK Bribery Act.

options.text: The US FCPA expressly exempts facilitation payments; the UK Bribery Act has no such exception
options.feedback: Correct. The US FCPA exempts small payments for routine governmental action. The UK Bribery Act has no facilitation payment exception.

options.text: The UK Bribery Act permits facilitation payments below £25
options.feedback: There is no de minimis monetary threshold.

options.text: Both treat facilitation payments as criminal with no exceptions
options.feedback: The US FCPA does have an express facilitation payment exception.

question: What is the primary tactical response for a company discovering it has made a facilitation payment?

options.text: Conceal the payment and enhance future compliance
options.feedback: Concealing is itself a criminal offence and aggravates any prosecution.

options.text: Self-report to the SFO, demonstrate remediation, and rely on prosecutorial discretion
options.feedback: Correct. The SFO/CPS Guidance indicates prosecution is less likely where the matter is self-reported, with no prior convictions and remediation.

options.text: Claim the payment was a legitimate business expense for tax purposes
options.feedback: Claiming a bribe as a business expense is a separate offence.

options.text: Argue the payment was lawful under local law
options.feedback: Local lawfulness is not a defence under the Bribery Act 2010.

oscoa_references: SFO/CPS Joint Prosecution Guidance on the Bribery Act 2010

question: 'The Bribery Act 2010 contains a de minimis exception for facilitation payments under £50.' — True or False?

options.text: True
options.feedback: The Bribery Act 2010 has no de minimis exception — all facilitation payments are potentially criminal.

options.text: False
options.feedback: Correct. The UK Bribery Act contains no monetary de minimis exception for facilitation payments.

question: Which statute provides the post-Brexit autonomous UK sanctions framework?

options.text: Policing and Crime Act 2017
options.feedback: PCA 2017 introduced civil monetary penalties, but the framework statute is SAMLA 2018.

options.text: Sanctions and Anti-Money Laundering Act 2018
options.feedback: Correct. SAMLA 2018 provides the legal framework for the UK's autonomous post-Brexit sanctions regime.

options.text: Criminal Finances Act 2017
options.feedback: CFA 2017 deals with UWOs and account freezing, not the core sanctions framework.

options.text: Serious Crime Act 2007
options.feedback: SCA 2007 deals with encouraging or assisting crime, not sanctions.

question: Under s 146 Policing and Crime Act 2017, OFSI may impose civil penalties on what basis?

options.text: On proof of knowledge or reasonable cause to suspect
options.feedback: This is the criminal standard, not civil.

options.text: On a strict liability basis — knowledge or reasonable cause to suspect is not required
options.feedback: Correct. s 146 PCA 2017 — civil monetary penalties are strict liability. Criminal prosecution requires knowledge/reasonable cause to suspect (SAMLA 2018, s 13).

options.text: Only on proof beyond reasonable doubt
options.feedback: Civil penalties are on the balance of probabilities.

options.text: Only where criminal prosecution has been unsuccessful
options.feedback: Civil and criminal sanctions are parallel enforcement options.

oscoa_references: Sanctions and Anti-Money Laundering Act 2018, s 13

question: What key distinction exists between criminal and civil liability for UK sanctions breaches?

options.text: Civil penalties require proof of dishonesty; criminal prosecution does not
options.feedback: Neither requires proof of dishonesty.

options.text: Criminal prosecution requires knowledge or reasonable cause to suspect; civil penalties are strict liability
options.feedback: Correct. Criminal liability under SAMLA 2018 s 13 requires knowledge/reasonable cause to suspect. Civil penalties under PCA 2017 s 146 are strict liability — a key tactical point for defence.

options.text: There is no distinction — both are judged on the same standard
options.feedback: The standards differ significantly.

options.text: Criminal applies only to UK nationals; civil applies to all
options.feedback: Both apply to all within UK jurisdiction.

oscoa_references: Sanctions and Anti-Money Laundering Act 2018, s 13

question: Which is a recognised sanctions evasion technique?

options.text: Filing a SAR with the NCA before a transaction
options.feedback: Filing a SAR is a compliance measure, not an evasion technique.

options.text: Using decentralised cryptocurrency exchanges and non-custodial wallets to circumvent asset freezes
options.feedback: Correct. DEXs, unhosted wallets, and privacy coins to evade financial controls is a recognised evasion technique.

options.text: Registering with OFSI before conducting international transactions
options.feedback: OFSI registration is a compliance step, not evasion.

options.text: Obtaining an OFSI licence for a specific transaction
options.feedback: Licensing is a lawful route, not an evasion technique.

question: Routing restricted goods through a non-sanctioned third country before onward shipment to a sanctioned destination is known as:

options.text: Triangulation
options.feedback: While conceptually similar, the specific trade sanctions term is different.

options.text: Transhipment
options.feedback: Correct. Transhipment — routing goods through a third country to obscure the ultimate destination and evade trade sanctions.

options.text: Circumvention by proxy
options.feedback: This is not the standard term in sanctions compliance.

options.text: Parallel importation
options.feedback: Parallel importation concerns IP and territorial licensing, not sanctions evasion.

question: Under s 52 of the Criminal Justice Act 1993, the insider dealing offence is committed where a person:

options.text: Deals in any securities while possessing any non-public information
options.feedback: The information must be 'inside information' as defined, and the person must be an 'insider'.

options.text: Deals in price-affected securities on a regulated market while in possession of inside information as an insider
options.feedback: Correct. s 52 CJA 1993 — dealing in price-affected securities on a regulated market while an insider in possession of inside information.

options.text: Provides financial advice without FCA authorisation
options.feedback: Unauthorised advice is a regulatory matter, not insider dealing under CJA 1993.

options.text: Short sells securities on any exchange worldwide
options.feedback: Short selling alone is not insider dealing — the possession of inside information is the key.

question: Under s 56 CJA 1993, inside information is information that is:

options.text: Any information about a company that is not in the public domain
options.feedback: The definition is more precise than simply 'not public'.

options.text: Specific or precise, not generally available, and would if made public be likely to have a significant effect on the price of securities
options.feedback: Correct. s 56 — inside information must be (a) specific/precise, (b) not generally available, (c) likely to significantly affect price if made public.

options.text: Any information held by a company director
options.feedback: Not all information held by directors qualifies as inside information.

options.text: Only information about takeover bids and mergers
options.feedback: Inside information covers a broader range than takeovers and mergers.

question: Under ss 53-54 and Schedule 1 CJA 1993, which is a defence to insider dealing?

options.text: The defendant did not profit from the transaction
options.feedback: Profit is not an element of the offence; lack of profit is not a defence.

options.text: The defendant would have dealt in the securities even without the inside information
options.feedback: Correct. s 53(1)(c) and Schedule 1 — a defence exists where D would have done what they did even if they had not had the inside information.

options.text: The defendant disclosed the information to the FCA before trading
options.feedback: Prior disclosure to the FCA is not a statutory defence.

options.text: The securities were purchased for less than £10,000
options.feedback: There is no de minimis threshold for insider dealing.

question: Under the Market Abuse Regulation (EU 596/2014 — retained EU law), the FCA can enforce against:

options.text: Criminal market abuse only, through the criminal courts
options.feedback: MAR provides for both criminal and civil enforcement.

options.text: Insider dealing, unlawful disclosure of inside information, and market manipulation — through civil fines, prohibition orders, and restitution
options.feedback: Correct. MAR provides the FCA with civil enforcement powers including fines, prohibition orders, and restitution for market abuse.

options.text: Only market manipulation by listed companies
options.feedback: The scope extends to individuals and across multiple categories of market abuse.

options.text: Only activities on the London Stock Exchange
options.feedback: MAR applies across UK regulated markets, not only the LSE.

oscoa_references: Financial Services and Markets Act 2000, Part VII

question: Under MAR Article 13, a defence to market manipulation exists where:

options.text: The behaviour was negligent but not intentional
options.feedback: Negligence is not the standard for the defence.

options.text: The behaviour falls within accepted market practices established by the FCA
options.feedback: Correct. MAR Art 13 provides a defence where the transaction/behaviour falls within an accepted market practice established by the competent authority.

options.text: The FCA has granted a specific exemption for the transaction
options.feedback: The defence is based on accepted market practices, not individual exemptions.

options.text: The amount involved is below £100,000
options.feedback: There is no de minimis threshold for market abuse.

question: Under POCA 2002 Part 2, a confiscation order is made:

options.text: Only where the defendant has been convicted of a drug trafficking offence
options.feedback: Drug trafficking is one trigger for the criminal lifestyle test, but confiscation is broader.

options.text: Following conviction, requiring the defendant to pay the recoverable amount of their benefit from criminal conduct
options.feedback: Correct. POCA 2002 Part 2 — a confiscation order requires payment of the recoverable amount following conviction.

options.text: Before conviction, based on suspicion of criminal property
options.feedback: Confiscation follows conviction; civil recovery (Part 5) does not require conviction.

options.text: Only for offences committed after 2010
options.feedback: POCA 2002 has been in force since 2003 and applies to offences after its commencement.

question: Under s 75 and Schedule 2 POCA 2002, a defendant has a 'criminal lifestyle' if they have been convicted of:

options.text: Any offence, provided they have three or more previous convictions
options.feedback: The criminal lifestyle test is offence-specific, not based on number of convictions.

options.text: Drug trafficking, money laundering, directing terrorism, people trafficking, arms trafficking, counterfeiting, and other specified offences
options.feedback: Correct. s 75 and Schedule 2 list specific offence categories triggering the criminal lifestyle assumptions.

options.text: Any offence punishable by more than 7 years imprisonment
options.feedback: The test is not purely sentence-based — it depends on the category of offence.

options.text: Only offences committed as part of organised crime
options.feedback: The Schedule 2 offences are specific, not defined by organised crime membership.

oscoa_references: Proceeds of Crime Act 2002, s 75, Schedule 2

question: Under s 10 POCA 2002, where a defendant has a criminal lifestyle, what assumptions does the court make?

options.text: All property ever owned by the defendant represents criminal benefit
options.feedback: The assumptions are time-limited, not unlimited.

options.text: Property transferred to or obtained by the defendant and expenditure incurred in the 6 years before proceedings began represents criminal benefit
options.feedback: Correct. s 10 makes four statutory assumptions, including that property obtained/transferred and expenditure over the preceding 6 years was funded by criminal conduct.

options.text: Only property identified in the indictment represents criminal benefit
options.feedback: The assumptions extend beyond the specific offences charged.

options.text: Property obtained within 12 months of conviction represents criminal benefit
options.feedback: The assumption period is 6 years before the commencement of proceedings, not 12 months.

question: Under s 10(6) POCA 2002, a court may refrain from applying the statutory assumptions where:

options.text: The defendant's family would suffer hardship
options.feedback: Family hardship alone is not the statutory ground.

options.text: There would be a serious risk of injustice if the assumption were made
options.feedback: Correct. s 10(6) — the court must not apply an assumption if satisfied there would be a serious risk of injustice. This is a key tactical argument in confiscation proceedings.

options.text: The defendant has no previous convictions
options.feedback: Lack of previous convictions does not automatically displace the assumptions.

options.text: The confiscated amount would exceed £1 million
options.feedback: There is no monetary threshold for the serious risk of injustice exception.

question: Under POCA 2002 Part 5, civil recovery of property obtained through unlawful conduct:

options.text: Requires a criminal conviction before recovery can begin
options.feedback: Part 5 is explicitly designed to operate without a conviction requirement.

options.text: Does not require a criminal conviction; recovery is on the balance of probabilities
options.feedback: Correct. POCA 2002 Part 5 allows civil recovery through the High Court on the civil standard of proof (balance of probabilities), without any conviction.

options.text: Applies only to cash over £10,000
options.feedback: Civil recovery applies to all forms of property, not just cash.

options.text: Is only available for terrorism-related property
options.feedback: Part 5 applies to property obtained through any unlawful conduct.

question: Account Freezing Orders and Forfeiture Orders were introduced by:

options.text: Serious Crime Act 2007
options.feedback: The SCA 2007 dealt with encouraging or assisting crime, not account freezing.

options.text: Criminal Finances Act 2017, amending POCA 2002
options.feedback: Correct. The CFA 2017 inserted ss 303Z1-303Z2 into POCA 2002, creating Account Freezing Orders and Forfeiture Orders.

options.text: Sanctions and Anti-Money Laundering Act 2018
options.feedback: SAMLA 2018 is the sanctions framework, not the source of account freezing powers.

options.text: Bribery Act 2010
options.feedback: The Bribery Act 2010 deals with bribery, not asset freezing.

question: What is the key tactical concern with civil recovery compared to confiscation proceedings?

options.text: Civil recovery has a higher standard of proof than confiscation
options.feedback: The opposite is true — civil recovery uses a lower standard of proof.

options.text: Civil recovery uses the balance of probabilities (not beyond reasonable doubt) and does not require a conviction — making it broader and easier for authorities to obtain
options.feedback: Correct. Part 5 civil recovery is available on the civil standard without any conviction, making it a powerful tool for authorities and a significant risk for respondents.

options.text: Civil recovery is limited to sums under £50,000
options.feedback: There is no such limit on civil recovery.

options.text: Civil recovery only applies where the respondent consents
options.feedback: Civil recovery proceedings are adversarial; respondent consent is not required.

question: Under the Criminal Finances Act 2017, ss 362A-362T, an Unexplained Wealth Order may be obtained against:

options.text: Any person with property exceeding £50,000 in the UK
options.feedback: Additional jurisdictional and status criteria apply.

options.text: A politically exposed person (non-EEA) or a person suspected of involvement in serious crime, where property exceeds £50,000
options.feedback: Correct. CFA 2017 requires the respondent to be (a) a non-EEA PEP, or (b) suspected of involvement in serious crime, and the property must exceed £50,000.

options.text: Only foreign nationals who are not UK residents
options.feedback: Residence status is not the defining criterion.

options.text: Any UK PEP, regardless of property value
options.feedback: The UWO regime is limited to non-EEA PEPs (or those suspected of serious crime) and property over £50,000.

question: What limitations in the UWO regime create tactical opportunities for resisting an order?

options.text: UWOs can be challenged only through judicial review
options.feedback: Challenges can be mounted on multiple grounds, not only judicial review.

options.text: The non-EEA PEP definition is narrow, and the £50,000 threshold limits application — arguments over PEP status, property valuation, and human rights (Art 8/Art 1 Protocol 1) provide grounds to resist
options.feedback: Correct. The UWO regime has definitional limitations (non-EEA PEP, £50,000 threshold) and may be challenged on human rights grounds — providing significant tactical opportunities.

options.text: UWOs cannot be enforced against UK nationals
options.feedback: UWOs apply to all persons, not only non-nationals.

options.text: UWOs require proof beyond reasonable doubt of criminal conduct
options.feedback: UWOs are obtained on a civil standard and do not require proof of specific criminal conduct.

oscoa_references: Human Rights Act 1998, Art 8, Protocol 1 Art 1

question: Under the Crime and Courts Act 2013, Schedule 17, a Deferred Prosecution Agreement is available for:

options.text: Individuals and corporations charged with any offence
options.feedback: DPAs are corporate only and for specified economic crimes.

options.text: Corporations (not individuals) for specified economic crimes including fraud, bribery, and money laundering
